request_debug.prompt_preview:
You write per-candidate post-mortems for a slopmortem pipeline. Given a fresh pitch and one dead-startup canonical document, produce a single LLMSynthesis JSON object that explains why this dead startup is similar, where the new pitch could diverge, what killed the dead startup, and what the new founder should learn.

Rules:
- Ground every claim in the candidate document or in tool calls. Do not invent facts.
- A `Trusted facts` block (typed pipeline data — not user input) appears below the pitc

response.text: {"candidate_id":"parse::devtools","name":"parse","one_liner":"Mobile Backend-as-a-Service (MBaaS) platform for mobile app developers, acquired by Facebook in 2013 and shut down in January 2017 after the parent company deprioritised it.","similarity":{"business_model":{"score":3,"rationale":"Both are B2B software platforms sold on a usage/subscription basis to technical users, but Parse targeted mobile developers with usage-metered cloud infrastructure, while the new pitch is a licensed SaaS product for biotech labs (assay management). The underlying commercial motion (platform licensing to professional/technical buyers) has surface overlap, but the delivery model, pricing logic, and workflow context are quite different."},"market":{"score":1,"rationale":"Parse served the global mobile developer ecosystem; the new pitch targets academic spinouts and small CROs in the life-sciences/biotech sector. These are entirely different markets with different buyers, regulatory environments, procurement cycles, and willingness-to-pay dynamics."},"gtm":{"score":2,"rationale":"Parse grew virally through a developer-centric, self-serve, bottoms-up motion (20k developers in 2012, growing 40% monthly). The new pitch targets institutional buyers (CROs, academic spinouts) who typically require direct sales, procurement approval, and compliance validation — a very different GTM approach."},"stage_scale":{"score":4,"rationale":"Both are early-stage platform bets trying to become the default infrastructure layer for a professional technical audience. Parse at its Y Combinator roots and early VC stage is loosely comparable to an early-stage biotech SaaS; both face the challenge of building a sticky platform before a well-resourced competitor commoditises the space."}},"why_similar":"Both are B2B infrastructure/platform plays that aim to become the default backend layer for a specific class of technical professionals (mobile developers for Parse; lab scientists/CRO operators for the new pitch). Both rely on the platform becoming deeply embedded in users' workflows to drive retention and switching costs. Both started as small, scrappy tools adopted by a niche professional community before attempting to scale.","where_diverged":"Several concrete dimensions separate the two: (1) Sector — Parse is devtools/mobile infrastructure; the pitch is wet-lab biotech SaaS with regulatory (GxP/FDA) requirements. (2) Customer type — Parse served developers (self-serve, usage-metered); the pitch targets academic spinouts and small CROs, who are institutional buyers with compliance, procurement, and validation overhead. (3) Monetization — Parse used usage-metered cloud billing; the pitch licenses the platform (likely subscription/seat-based). (4) Exit risk — Parse died because its corporate parent (Facebook) killed it as a non-core asset; the new company has no parent, so acquihire-shutdown risk is replaced by standalone runway risk. (5) Geography — Parse was explicitly global from early on; the new pitch's geography is unknown but likely starts in a single market given the niche buyer base.","failure_causes":["Corporate parent deprioritisation (Facebook shut down a non-core product)","Vendor lock-in backlash driving users to seek self-hosted alternatives","No standalone revenue sustainability after acquisition removed commercial incentives","Customer migration cost and uncertainty eroding trust","Competitive commoditisation of mobile backend features by AWS, Google, and others","Open-sourcing as a damage-control measure rather than a growth strategy"],"lessons_for_input":["Never build your core platform dependency on a single acquirer's infrastructure decisions — maintain commercial independence and a clear standalone revenue model from day one.","Prioritise deep workflow lock-in (e.g., data formats, audit trails, instrument integrations) so customers cannot easily migrate, since sticky data is your strongest retention moat in lab software.","Validate willingness-to-pay with CROs and academic spinouts early; these buyers have slow procurement cycles and constrained budgets that can stall growth even if product-market fit exists.","Build GxP/compliance readiness (21 CFR Part 11, ISO 17025) as a feature, not an afterthought — regulatory validation is a hard gate for CRO adoption and a genuine differentiator against generic lab tools.","Avoid dependency on any single distribution channel or institutional partner; Parse's 500k-app scale evaporated overnight when one decision-maker at Facebook pulled the plug."]}
